title: Non-overlapping intervals
description: Find minimum intervals to remove to eliminate overlaps

examples.explanation: Intervals: [1,2], [2,3], [3,4], [1,3]. Total intervals = 4. Recursively explore subsets: Start with index=0, included=[]. Include [1,2]: included=[[1,2]], index=1. Include [2,3]: no overlap with [1,2] (2>=2? 2>=2 is true, but intervals touch at point 2, typically considered non-overlapping if end equals start), so included=[[1,2],[2,3]], index=2. Include [3,4]: no overlap, included=[[1,2],[2,3],[3,4]], index=3. Include [1,3]: overlaps with [1,2] (1<2 and 3>1) so cannot include; try excluding. Recurse back: exclude [3,4] and try other combinations. Maximum non-overlapping found: subset [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4]] size=3, or [[1,2],[3,4]] size=2, etc. Best size=3. Removals = 4-3=1.

Intervals: three identical [1,2]. Total=3. All overlap each other (same intervals). Explore subsets: Only subsets with at most one interval are non-overlapping. Maximum non-overlapping size=1 (e.g., keep just one interval). Removals = 3-1=2.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all subsets of intervals using recursion or bitmasking, check each for overlaps, and track the minimum removals needed. Use this only for small input sizes due to exponential growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Minimum Interval Removal
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion to generate all subsets and find minimum removals to eliminate overlaps.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the non-overlapping intervals problem. The intuition is that we need to consider all possible subsets of intervals to find which ones to keep (or remove) such that there are no overlaps. Since we want to minimize removals, we want to maximize the number of intervals we can keep without overlaps. The brute force approach explores every possible combination of intervals using recursion, checking each subset for overlaps, and tracking the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals we can keep. The minimum removals is then total intervals minus this maximum. The algorithm works by recursively including or excluding each interval, building subsets, and checking overlaps only when we reach a leaf of the recursion tree. At each step, if we include an interval, we must ensure it doesn't overlap with previously included intervals in the current subset. We use a backtracking approach: try including an interval if it doesn't conflict, then recursively process the next interval; also try excluding it. This ensures we explore all 2^n subsets. The time complexity is O(2^n * n) in worst case because we check overlaps for each subset (which takes O(n) time per subset), but with pruning we can optimize overlap checks. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and storing the current subset. Connection to L1: This uses the overlap checking subroutine from L1 to validate subsets. Common mistakes include not pruning early (checking overlaps as we build subsets to avoid exploring invalid paths), forgetting to handle empty subsets, or incorrectly calculating removals. Real code pattern: Use recursion with index parameter, maintain list of included intervals, and update best count.

examples.trace: Intervals: [1,2], [2,3], [3,4], [1,3]. Total intervals = 4. Recursively explore subsets: Start with index=0, included=[]. Include [1,2]: included=[[1,2]], index=1. Include [2,3]: no overlap with [1,2] (2>=2? 2>=2 is true, but intervals touch at point 2, typically considered non-overlapping if end equals start), so included=[[1,2],[2,3]], index=2. Include [3,4]: no overlap, included=[[1,2],[2,3],[3,4]], index=3. Include [1,3]: overlaps with [1,2] (1<2 and 3>1) so cannot include; try excluding. Recurse back: exclude [3,4] and try other combinations. Maximum non-overlapping found: subset [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4]] size=3, or [[1,2],[3,4]] size=2, etc. Best size=3. Removals = 4-3=1.

Intervals: three identical [1,2]. Total=3. All overlap each other (same intervals). Explore subsets: Only subsets with at most one interval are non-overlapping. Maximum non-overlapping size=1 (e.g., keep just one interval). Removals = 3-1=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Overlap Checking for Intervals
ladders.desc: Function to determine if two intervals overlap or if a new interval overlaps with any in a set.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is crucial for the brute force approach because we need to validate subsets. The intuition is that two intervals [a,b] and [c,d] overlap if they share any common point in time. Mathematically, they overlap if a < d and c < b (assuming intervals are half-open [start, end) or inclusive based on problem definition; typically, if end equals start, they are considered non-overlapping). For the brute force, we check overlap between a new interval and all intervals already included in the current subset. The algorithm: given a list of intervals 'included' and a new interval 'new_interval', iterate through each interval in 'included' and check if new_interval overlaps with it using the condition: if new_interval[0] < existing[1] and existing[0] < new_interval[1], then they overlap. If any overlap is found, return True; else return False. Time complexity for checking one new interval against k included intervals is O(k). In the full recursion, this is called many times, but we can prune early. Space complexity is O(1) for the check. Connection to L0: This function is used in the recursion to decide whether to include an interval. Common mistakes: incorrect overlap condition (e.g., using <= instead of < for non-overlap when ends touch), not handling empty included list, or assuming sorted order. Real code pattern: Define function overlaps(interval1, interval2) and use it in a loop.

examples.trace: Check overlap between [1,3] and [2,4]: Condition: 2 < 3 and 1 < 4 -> 2<3 is true, 1<4 is true, so overlap exists. Return True.
Check [2,3] with [1,2]: 2 < 2? 2<2 is false (if using strict <, no overlap since end equals start). Check [2,3] with [3,4]: 2 < 4 and 3 < 3? 3<3 is false. So no overlap with either. Return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Subset Generation with Backtracking
ladders.desc: Generate all subsets of intervals using recursion and track the best non-overlapping subset size.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive mechanism for exploring all combinations. The intuition is that to find the maximum non-overlapping subset, we need to consider every possible selection of intervals. We use a depth-first search (DFS) recursion: at each step (for each interval index), we have two choices: include the interval in the current subset or exclude it. If we include, we must ensure it doesn't overlap with already included intervals (using the overlap check from L1). We recursively call the function for the next index, updating the list of included intervals and the count. We also keep a global or passed-along variable to track the maximum size of non-overlapping subsets found so far. The base case is when we've processed all intervals (index == n), at which point we update the maximum if the current subset size is larger. The algorithm uses backtracking: after trying to include an interval, we remove it (backtrack) to try the exclude path. This ensures all 2^n subsets are considered. Time complexity is O(2^n) due to exploring all subsets, with O(n) space for recursion depth and the included list. Connection to L0: This recursion is the main driver of the brute force solution. Common mistakes: not backtracking correctly (forgetting to pop the included interval), not pruning invalid includes early (check overlap before including), or inefficiently recalculating overlaps. Real code pattern: Define recursive function dfs(idx, included) that updates a global max_count.

examples.trace: Start: idx=0, included=[], max=0. Include [1,2]: included=[[1,2]], idx=1. Include [2,3]: check overlap with [1,2] -> no overlap (2>=2? if end==start non-overlap), so include: included=[[1,2],[2,3]], idx=2 (base case). Update max=2. Backtrack: remove [2,3], included=[[1,2]], idx=1. Exclude [2,3]: idx=2, included=[[1,2]], update max=1. Backtrack more: remove [1,2], included=[], idx=0. Exclude [1,2]: idx=1, included=[]. Include [2,3]: included=[[2,3]], idx=2, update max=1. Exclude [2,3]: idx=2, included=[], update max=0. Final max=2.

Start: idx=0, included=[], max=0. Include [1,2]: included=[[1,2]], idx=1. Include [1,3]: check overlap with [1,2] -> overlaps (1<2 and 1<3), so cannot include; try exclude. Exclude [1,3]: idx=2, included=[[1,2]], update max=1. Backtrack: remove [1,2], included=[], idx=0. Exclude [1,2]: idx=1, included=[]. Include [1,3]: included=[[1,3]], idx=2, update max=1. Exclude [1,3]: idx=2, included=[], update max=0. Final max=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Representing and Comparing Intervals
ladders.desc: Understand interval data structure and basic comparisons for overlap detection.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves how intervals are represented and the fundamental comparison operations needed. Intervals are typically represented as pairs of numbers [start, end], where start <= end. The key operations are: checking if two intervals overlap, and comparing intervals for sorting or other purposes. For overlap detection, we need to understand the condition: intervals [a,b] and [c,d] overlap if they share any common point. Assuming intervals are half-open [start, end) (common in many problems), they overlap if a < d and c < b. If intervals are inclusive [start, end], the condition might be a <= d and c <= b, but often problems define non-overlap when end equals start. It's crucial to clarify the problem's definition. In our brute force approach, we use this condition to check overlaps. Additionally, we might sort intervals by start or end to optimize, but for brute force, we process in given order. The intuition is that interval comparison is based on their endpoints. Time complexity for comparing two intervals is O(1). Space is O(1). Connection to L2: This comparison is used in the overlap check within recursion. Common mistakes: misinterpreting overlap condition, not handling edge cases like equal intervals, or incorrect data types. Real code pattern: Store intervals as lists of two integers and define comparison functions.

examples.trace: Check condition: 1 < 4 and 2 < 3 -> 1<4 is true, 2<3 is true, so overlap exists.
Check condition: 1 < 3 and 2 < 2 -> 1<3 is true, 2<2 is false (since <, not <=), so no overlap.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through a List with Index
ladders.desc: Loop through a list of items using an index to access each element sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental programming operation where we traverse a list (or array) from start to end using an index variable. In the context of the brute force solution, we need to process each interval one by one in the recursion. The intuition is that we use an index (e.g., i) to point to the current interval being considered. We start at index 0 and increment it as we move to the next interval. In recursion, this index is passed as a parameter to track our position. The basic operation involves: initializing index to 0, checking if index is less than the list length (to avoid out-of-bounds), accessing the element at that index (e.g., intervals[i]), and then incrementing the index (e.g., i+1) for the next recursive call. This allows us to systematically consider each interval in order. Time complexity for iterating through n items is O(n), but in recursion it's part of the exponential exploration. Space complexity is O(1) for the index variable. Connection to L3: This iteration is used to access intervals for comparison and inclusion. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., using <= instead of < for length), not updating index correctly, or modifying the list while iterating. Real code pattern: Use a for-loop or while-loop with index, or in recursion use a parameter idx.

examples.trace: Index=0: access list[0] = [1,2]. Increment index to 1. Index=1: access list[1] = [3,4]. Increment index to 2. Index=2 equals length, stop.
Index=0, length=0, so index >= length, stop immediately.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Lists and Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic concept of a list as an ordered collection and how to access elements using indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding what a list (or array) is and how to use indices to get elements. A list is an ordered collection of items, where each item has a position called an index. In most programming languages, indices start at 0. For example, if we have a list intervals = [[1,2], [3,4]], then intervals[0] gives the first element [1,2], and intervals[1] gives the second [3,4]. The length of the list is the number of items, accessible via len(intervals). We can iterate over the list using a loop that goes from index 0 to len(intervals)-1. This foundational concept is essential because in the brute force solution, we store intervals in a list and access them by index during recursion. The intuition is that lists allow us to group related data (intervals) and process them in sequence. Time complexity for accessing an element by index is O(1). Space complexity for storing n intervals is O(n). Connection to L4: This basic knowledge enables the iteration operation. Common mistakes: confusing indices (thinking index 1 is the first item), accessing out-of-bounds indices, or not understanding that lists can hold complex objects like pairs. Real code pattern: Define a list, use square brackets to access, and use len() to get size.

examples.trace: List has two elements. Index 0 refers to the first element: [1,2]. Index 1 refers to the second: [3,4]. Length is 2.
List has no elements, so any index access like intervals[0] would cause an error. Length is 0.

approaches.name: Greedy Sort by Start Time O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Sort intervals by start time, iterate through them, and remove overlapping intervals by keeping track of the last non-overlapping interval's end. This approach prioritizes early starts but may not always yield the minimum removals without additional checks.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Intervals to Remove for Non-Overlapping Intervals
ladders.desc: Complete solution using greedy sort by start time to find minimum intervals to remove
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum number of intervals to remove so that no intervals overlap. The greedy approach sorts intervals by start time, then iterates through them while tracking the end of the last kept interval. When we encounter an overlap, we remove the interval with the later end time to minimize future overlaps. The intuition is that by always keeping the interval that ends earlier when there's a conflict, we maximize the space available for subsequent intervals. This works because intervals are sorted by start time - when two intervals overlap, the one ending later is more likely to overlap with future intervals. The algorithm: 1) Sort intervals by start time (O(n log n)). 2) Initialize count = 0 and last_end = -inf. 3) For each interval [start, end]: if start < last_end (overlap), increment count and update last_end = min(last_end, end) (keep the earlier ending interval). Else, update last_end = end (no overlap, keep current interval). 4) Return count. Time: O(n log n) for sorting, O(n) for iteration = O(n log n). Space: O(1) if sorting in-place, O(n) if not. Connection to L1: This uses the overlap detection and resolution logic from L1 in a complete solution. Common mistakes: Not sorting properly, incorrectly handling equal start times, forgetting to update last_end when keeping an interval. Real code pattern: def eraseOverlapIntervals(intervals): intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0]); count = 0; last_end = float('-inf'); for start, end in intervals: if start < last_end: count += 1; last_end = min(last_end, end); else: last_end = end; return count

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort intervals by start: [[1,2],[1,3],[2,3],[3,4]]. Step 2: Initialize count=0, last_end=-inf. Step 3: Process [1,2]: start=1, last_end=-inf → no overlap, keep it, last_end=2. Step 4: Process [1,3]: start=1, last_end=2 → overlap (1<2), count=1, keep earlier ending interval: min(2,3)=2, last_end=2. Step 5: Process [2,3]: start=2, last_end=2 → no overlap (2 not < 2), keep it, last_end=3. Step 6: Process [3,4]: start=3, last_end=3 → no overlap (3 not < 3), keep it, last_end=4. Step 7: Return count=1.

Step 1: Sort intervals: [[1,2],[1,2],[1,2]]. Step 2: count=0, last_end=-inf. Step 3: Process first [1,2]: no overlap, keep, last_end=2. Step 4: Process second [1,2]: start=1, last_end=2 → overlap, count=1, last_end=min(2,2)=2. Step 5: Process third [1,2]: start=1, last_end=2 → overlap, count=2, last_end=min(2,2)=2. Step 6: Return count=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Detect and Resolve Interval Overlaps
ladders.desc: Helper function to identify overlapping intervals and decide which to keep
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core decision-making logic within the greedy algorithm. Given two intervals where one starts before the other ends (overlap), we must decide which interval to keep to minimize future overlaps. The key insight: when intervals are sorted by start time, and we encounter an overlap between the current interval and the last kept interval, we should keep the interval that ends earlier. This is because the interval ending later extends further into the future, increasing the chance of overlapping with subsequent intervals. The algorithm: 1) Input: current interval [curr_start, curr_end] and last_end (end of last kept interval). 2) Check overlap: if curr_start < last_end, then overlap exists. 3) If overlap: increment removal count, update last_end = min(last_end, curr_end) (keep earlier ending). 4) If no overlap: update last_end = curr_end (keep current). Time: O(1) per comparison. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This logic is repeated for each interval in the main loop. Connection to L2: This implements the greedy choice principle. Common mistakes: Using <= instead of < for overlap check (intervals touching at endpoints are not overlapping), incorrectly updating last_end. Real code pattern: if curr_start < last_end: count += 1; last_end = min(last_end, curr_end); else: last_end = curr_end

examples.trace: Step 1: Check overlap: curr_start=1, last_end=2 → 1<2 → overlap. Step 2: Increment removal count. Step 3: Update last_end = min(2,3)=2. Step 4: Result: keep interval ending at 2 (the earlier ending one).
Step 1: Check overlap: curr_start=4, last_end=3 → 4 not < 3 → no overlap. Step 2: No removal. Step 3: Update last_end = curr_end=5. Step 4: Result: keep current interval [4,5].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Choice: Keep Earlier Ending Intervals
ladders.desc: The central greedy principle of preferring intervals that end earlier
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental insight that makes the algorithm work. When faced with overlapping intervals sorted by start time, always keep the one that ends earlier. Why does this work? Consider two overlapping intervals A and B where A starts first (since sorted). If we keep A, B might overlap with A but future intervals only need to avoid A's end. If we keep B, future intervals must avoid B's end, which is later than A's end, thus more restrictive. Mathematically, minimizing the end point of kept intervals maximizes available space for subsequent intervals. This is a local optimal choice that leads to a global optimum for this problem. The logic: given intervals sorted by start, maintain the end of the last kept interval. When a new interval overlaps, compare its end with the current last_end and keep the smaller end. This ensures we always have the earliest possible endpoint, reducing future conflicts. Time: O(1) per decision. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This principle guides the overlap resolution. Connection to L3: This relies on understanding interval comparisons. Common mistakes: Thinking we should keep the interval that starts earlier (already sorted), or keeping the shorter interval rather than earlier ending. Real code pattern: When overlap: last_end = min(last_end, curr_end)

examples.input: Interval A ends at 3, Interval B ends at 5, they overlap
examples.trace: Step 1: Intervals overlap (sorted by start). Step 2: Compare ends: 3 vs 5. Step 3: Choose smaller end: 3. Step 4: Keep interval ending at 3 to minimize future overlaps.

examples.input: Last kept ends at 4, new interval ends at 2, they overlap
examples.trace: Step 1: Overlap detected. Step 2: Compare ends: 4 vs 2. Step 3: Choose smaller end: 2. Step 4: Update to keep interval ending at 2, even though it's the new interval.

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing and Sorting Intervals by Start Time
ladders.desc: Sort intervals based on their start values to enable greedy processing
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves organizing intervals so we can process them in a meaningful order. Sorting by start time is crucial because it allows us to consider intervals in the order they begin, making overlap detection straightforward. When intervals are sorted by start, any overlap between consecutive intervals in the sorted list means the current interval starts before the previous one ends. The sorting ensures we always compare each interval with the most recent kept interval, simplifying the greedy choice. Algorithm: Use any O(n log n) sorting algorithm (like quicksort or mergesort) with a custom comparator that compares the first element of each interval. For two intervals [a,b] and [c,d], we compare a and c. If a == c, we might also sort by end (though not strictly needed). Time: O(n log n). Space: O(1) if in-place, O(n) if not. Connection to L2: Sorting enables the greedy principle to work correctly. Connection to L4: Uses comparison operations. Common mistakes: Sorting by end instead of start (works for a different greedy approach), not handling ties properly. Real code pattern: intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0]) or intervals.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], x[1]))

examples.trace: Step 1: List intervals: [3,4], [1,2], [2,3]. Step 2: Extract starts: 3, 1, 2. Step 3: Sort starts: 1, 2, 3. Step 4: Reorder intervals: [1,2], [2,3], [3,4].

Step 1: Intervals: [1,5], [1,3], [2,4]. Step 2: Starts: 1, 1, 2. Step 3: Sort by start: all with start=1 come first, then start=2. Step 4: Within start=1, order may vary if not secondary sort. Result typically: [1,3], [1,5], [2,4] if sorting by (start, end).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check if Two Intervals Overlap
ladders.desc: Determine whether two given intervals overlap in time
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks if two intervals share any common points in time. For intervals [a,b] and [c,d] (where a ≤ b and c ≤ d), they overlap if they are not completely separate. The key condition: the start of one interval is less than the end of the other interval. Specifically, intervals overlap if a < d and c < b. However, in our greedy algorithm where intervals are sorted by start (a ≤ c), we only need to check if c < b (the start of the second is less than the end of the first). If c < b, they overlap; otherwise, they don't. Note: we use strict inequality (<) because intervals touching at endpoints (e.g., [1,2] and [2,3]) are considered non-overlapping in this problem. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is used during sorting and processing. Connection to L5: Builds on number comparisons. Common mistakes: Using ≤ instead of <, checking wrong pairs of values. Real code pattern: if start2 < end1: overlap = True else: overlap = False

examples.trace: Step 1: Interval1: [1,3], Interval2: [2,4]. Step 2: Since sorted by start, check if start2 < end1: 2 < 3 → True. Step 3: They overlap because 2 is less than 3.
Step 1: Interval1: [1,2], Interval2: [2,3]. Step 2: Check if start2 < end1: 2 < 2 → False. Step 3: No overlap because 2 is not less than 2 (they touch at point 2).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Intervals as Number Pairs
ladders.desc: Basic representation of intervals using start and end numbers
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding what an interval is and how to represent it. An interval is a continuous range of numbers between a start and an end point, inclusive or exclusive based on context. In this problem, we represent intervals as pairs [start, end] where start and end are numbers (typically integers), and start ≤ end. The interval includes all numbers from start to end, but for overlap checking, we consider it as covering the range. For example, [1,3] represents numbers 1, 2, 3 if inclusive. Key concepts: 1) Start is the beginning point. 2) End is the ending point. 3) Length = end - start. 4) Two intervals are equal if both start and end match. This foundation is essential because all operations (sorting, comparing, overlap checking) work on these start and end values. Time: O(1) to access start/end. Space: O(1) per interval. Connection to L4: Provides the data structure for overlap checks. Common mistakes: Assuming start > end, misunderstanding inclusive/exclusive boundaries. Real code pattern: Represent as list of two elements: interval = [start, end]; start = interval[0]; end = interval[1]

examples.trace: Step 1: Given interval [2,5]. Step 2: Start is the first number: 2. Step 3: End is the second number: 5. Step 4: Length = end - start = 5 - 2 = 3. Step 5: This interval covers numbers 2,3,4,5 (if inclusive).
Step 1: Interval [0,0]. Step 2: Start = 0. Step 3: End = 0. Step 4: Length = 0 - 0 = 0. Step 5: This is a zero-length interval at point 0.

approaches.name: Greedy Sort by End Time O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Sort intervals by end time, iterate through them, and count removals by comparing each interval's start with the last kept interval's end. This is optimal for minimizing removals as it maximizes non-overlapping intervals.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Intervals to Remove for Non-Overlapping Intervals
ladders.desc: Complete solution using greedy sort by end time to find minimum removals
ladders.explanation: This problem asks us to find the minimum number of intervals to remove so that no intervals overlap. The greedy approach sorts intervals by their end time, then iterates through them while keeping track of the last kept interval's end. When we encounter an interval that starts before the last kept interval ends, it overlaps and we need to remove it. By always keeping the interval with the earliest end time when we encounter overlaps, we maximize the number of intervals we can keep, which minimizes the number we need to remove. This works because if we have two overlapping intervals, keeping the one that ends earlier leaves more room for subsequent intervals. The algorithm: 1) Sort intervals by end time (O(n log n)). 2) Initialize count = 0 (removals) and last_end = -infinity. 3) For each interval [start, end]: if start < last_end, increment count (remove this interval); else update last_end = end (keep this interval). 4) Return count. Time: O(n log n) for sorting, O(n) for iteration = O(n log n). Space: O(1) if sorting in-place, O(n) if not. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of comparing intervals during iteration. Common mistakes: Not sorting by end time (sorting by start time gives wrong results), incorrect comparison (should be start < last_end, not <= unless intervals touching are considered overlapping), forgetting to handle empty input. Real code pattern: def minRemovals(intervals): intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1]); count = 0; last_end = float('-inf'); for start, end in intervals: if start < last_end: count += 1; else: last_end = end; return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort by end time: [[1,2],[2,3],[1,3],[3,4]] (ends: 2,3,3,4). Step 2: Initialize count=0, last_end=-inf. Step 3: Iterate: [1,2]: start=1 < -inf? No, keep, last_end=2. [2,3]: start=2 < 2? No, keep, last_end=3. [1,3]: start=1 < 3? Yes, remove, count=1. [3,4]: start=3 < 3? No, keep, last_end=4. Step 4: Return count=1.

Step 1: Sort by end time: [[1,2],[1,2],[1,2]] (all end at 2). Step 2: count=0, last_end=-inf. Step 3: Iterate: [1,2]: start=1 < -inf? No, keep, last_end=2. [1,2]: start=1 < 2? Yes, remove, count=1. [1,2]: start=1 < 2? Yes, remove, count=2. Step 4: Return count=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative Overlap Detection with Last End Tracking
ladders.desc: The core iteration that compares each interval with the last kept interval
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the greedy algorithm. After sorting intervals by end time, we need to traverse them and decide which to keep or remove. The key insight is that by always keeping the interval with the earliest end time when we encounter overlaps, we leave maximum room for future intervals. The algorithm: Initialize last_end to negative infinity (or a very small number). For each interval [start, end] in sorted order: if start < last_end, then this interval overlaps with the last kept interval, so we count it as a removal; otherwise, we keep this interval and update last_end = end. This comparison (start < last_end) checks if the current interval starts before the previous kept interval ends—if so, they overlap. The subroutine runs in O(n) time after sorting, with O(1) space for variables. Connection to L0: This is the main loop in the full solution. Connection to L2: It implements the core logic of greedy selection. Common mistakes: Using <= instead of < if intervals touching at endpoints are considered non-overlapping (problem specifies overlaps, so use <), not initializing last_end properly, or updating last_end when removing an interval. Real code pattern: def detectOverlaps(sorted_intervals): count = 0; last_end = float('-inf'); for start, end in sorted_intervals: if start < last_end: count += 1; else: last_end = end; return count.

examples.trace: Input is already sorted by end time. Step 1: last_end=-inf, count=0. Step 2: [1,2]: start=1 < -inf? No, keep, last_end=2. Step 3: [2,3]: start=2 < 2? No, keep, last_end=3. Step 4: [1,3]: start=1 < 3? Yes, remove, count=1. Step 5: [3,4]: start=3 < 3? No, keep, last_end=4. Step 6: Return count=1.

Step 1: last_end=-inf, count=0. Step 2: [1,2]: start=1 < -inf? No, keep, last_end=2. Step 3: [1,2]: start=1 < 2? Yes, remove, count=1. Step 4: [1,2]: start=1 < 2? Yes, remove, count=2. Step 5: Return count=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Selection by Earliest End Time
ladders.desc: The insight that picking intervals ending earliest maximizes non-overlapping count
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental greedy principle behind the algorithm. When we have a set of intervals, to maximize how many we can keep without overlaps, we should always select the interval that ends the earliest among the overlapping ones. Why does this work? Consider two overlapping intervals A and B where A ends before B. If we keep A, we free up the timeline sooner, allowing more potential intervals after A to fit without overlap. If we keep B instead, its later end time might block intervals that could have fit after A. This choice is locally optimal and leads to a globally optimal solution because at each decision point, picking the earliest-ending interval never worsens the overall outcome. The logic: Given sorted intervals by end time, iterate and always keep an interval if it doesn't overlap with the last kept one; if it overlaps, remove it. This ensures we keep intervals in order of increasing end times, maximizing count. Time: O(n) for the greedy pass, but relies on sorted input. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the iterative detection. Connection to L3: It builds on comparing intervals. Common mistakes: Not understanding why sorting by end time is crucial (sorting by start time doesn't guarantee optimality), or thinking a different greedy rule (like picking shortest interval) would work. Real code pattern: Concept only—no code, but the idea is: sort by end; keep if no overlap with last kept; else remove.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort by end: [[1,3],[2,4]] (ends at 3 and 4). Step 2: Start with last_end=-inf. [1,3]: no overlap, keep, last_end=3. [2,4]: start=2 < 3? Yes, overlaps, so remove. Greedy choice: kept [1,3] because it ends earlier than [2,4].

Step 1: Sort by end: [[2,3],[1,5],[4,6]] (ends: 3,5,6). Step 2: last_end=-inf. [2,3]: keep, last_end=3. [1,5]: start=1 < 3? Yes, overlaps, remove. [4,6]: start=4 < 3? No, keep, last_end=6. Greedy choice: kept [2,3] (ends at 3) over [1,5] (ends at 5) because earlier end allows [4,6] to fit.

ladders.title: Building Block — Interval Overlap Detection
ladders.desc: Determining if two intervals overlap based on their start and end times
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental skill needed for the problem: given two intervals [start1, end1] and [start2, end2], determine if they overlap. Intervals overlap if they share any common point in time. Mathematically, two intervals overlap if the start of one is less than the end of the other, and vice versa, but a simpler rule: they do NOT overlap if one ends before or at the start of the other. So, intervals overlap if start1 < end2 AND start2 < end1. In the greedy algorithm, we compare each interval with the last kept interval: if current start < last end, they overlap. Why? Because we sort by end time, so last end is the end of the last kept interval. If current starts before that ends, they must overlap. This detection runs in O(1) time. Connection to L2: Overlap detection is used in the greedy selection to decide whether to keep or remove. Connection to L4: It uses comparison operations on numbers. Common mistakes: Using <= or > incorrectly, forgetting to handle equal endpoints (if intervals touching are considered non-overlapping, use <= for non-overlap), or confusing start/end order. Real code pattern: def overlaps(interval1, interval2): start1, end1 = interval1; start2, end2 = interval2; return start1 < end2 and start2 < end1. For greedy: overlap = start < last_end.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if A starts before B ends: startA=1 < endB=4? Yes. Step 2: Check if B starts before A ends: startB=2 < endA=3? Yes. Step 3: Both conditions true, so they overlap. Alternatively, using greedy comparison: if sorted by end, A=[1,3], B=[2,4], last_end=3 after keeping A; for B, startB=2 < last_end=3? Yes, so overlap.

Step 1: Check if A starts before B ends: startA=1 < endB=3? Yes. Step 2: Check if B starts before A ends: startB=2 < endA=2? No (2 < 2 is false). Step 3: Not both true, so no overlap. In greedy: if last_end=2 after keeping A, for B, startB=2 < 2? No, so no overlap.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Sorting Intervals by End Time
ladders.desc: Sorting a list of intervals based on their end value
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation in the algorithm: given a list of intervals, each represented as [start, end], sort them in ascending order based on the end value. Sorting is necessary to apply the greedy principle of picking intervals that end earliest. In Python, this can be done using the sort() method with a key function: intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1]). This ensures that after sorting, intervals are ordered from smallest end to largest end. If two intervals have the same end time, the order doesn't matter for the greedy algorithm, but typically we might sort by start as a tie-breaker for consistency. Time complexity: O(n log n) for comparison-based sorting. Space: O(1) if sorting in-place, or O(n) if creating a new sorted list. Connection to L3: Sorted intervals are needed for efficient overlap detection in the greedy pass. Connection to L5: It uses comparison of numbers. Common mistakes: Sorting by start time instead of end (which leads to incorrect greedy decisions), not using a stable sort if order matters, or incorrectly accessing the end element. Real code pattern: intervals = [[1,3],[2,4],[1,2]]; intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1]); result: [[1,2],[1,3],[2,4]].

examples.trace: Step 1: Extract end values: 3,4,2. Step 2: Sort ends: 2,3,4. Step 3: Rearrange intervals in order of ends: interval with end=2 is [1,2], place first; end=3 is [1,3], place second; end=4 is [2,4], place third. Result: [[1,2],[1,3],[2,4]].
Step 1: Ends: 10,6,12. Step 2: Sort ends: 6,10,12. Step 3: Intervals in order: end=6 is [1,6]; end=10 is [5,10]; end=12 is [7,12]. Result: [[1,6],[5,10],[7,12]].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Comparing Numbers and Understanding Intervals
ladders.desc: Basic skills: comparing integers and representing intervals as pairs
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding how to compare numbers and what an interval represents. An interval is a continuous range on a number line, represented as a pair [start, end] where start and end are integers (or real numbers), and start <= end. For example, [1,3] represents all numbers from 1 to 3, inclusive. The key operations are comparing start and end values: we need to know if one number is less than, equal to, or greater than another. In the context of the problem, we'll be comparing starts and ends to check for overlaps or to sort. For instance, to say interval A ends before interval B starts, we check if endA <= startB. This foundation involves no algorithm—just basic math and data representation. Time: O(1) for comparisons. Space: O(1) for storing an interval. Connection to L4: These comparisons are used in sorting and overlap detection. Common mistakes: Misunderstanding interval notation (e.g., thinking [1,3] includes only 1 and 3), incorrect inequality signs, or not handling edge cases like empty intervals. Real code pattern: Represent interval as list or tuple: interval = [start, end]; compare: if start1 < end2: ...

examples.input: Interval: [2,5], Check: Is start < end? Is 3 within interval?
examples.trace: Step 1: For interval [2,5], start=2, end=5. Step 2: Compare start and end: 2 < 5 is true, so valid interval. Step 3: Check if 3 is within: 3 >= 2 and 3 <= 5, so true.

examples.input: Compare: Which is smaller, end of [1,4] or start of [3,6]?
examples.output: End of [1,4] is 4, start of [3,6] is 3; 4 > 3
examples.trace: Step 1: For [1,4], end=4. Step 2: For [3,6], start=3. Step 3: Compare 4 and 3: 4 > 3, so end of first is greater than start of second.

testCases.input: Compare: end of [1,3] (which is 3) and start of [4,6] (which is 4)
Interval: [a,b] where a=6, b=2, Is a <= b?
Interval: [100,200], What is the length? (end - start)
Compare: start of [0,5] (0) and end of [1,4] (4)

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Sort intervals by start time, then apply a longest increasing subsequence (LIS) style DP to find the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals. The minimum removals is total intervals minus this maximum.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Intervals to Remove for Non-Overlap
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP O(n²) approach to find minimum intervals to remove
ladders.explanation: This problem asks us to find the minimum number of intervals to remove so that no intervals overlap. The DP O(n²) approach works by first sorting intervals by start time, then finding the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals using a longest increasing subsequence (LIS) style DP. The intuition is that if we can find the maximum number of intervals we can keep without overlap, then the minimum to remove is simply total intervals minus this maximum. Sorting by start time ensures we process intervals in chronological order, making it easier to check compatibility. The DP array dp[i] stores the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals ending at interval i. For each interval i, we check all previous intervals j (j < i) and if intervals[i] doesn't overlap with intervals[j], we can extend the sequence. The recurrence is dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1) for all j where intervals[i] doesn't overlap intervals[j]. After computing all dp values, the maximum value in dp gives us the maximum non-overlapping intervals we can keep. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops, and space is O(n) for the dp array. Common mistakes include not sorting properly, incorrect overlap check (should be intervals[i].start >= intervals[j].end), and forgetting to initialize dp[i] = 1 for all i. This connects to L1 by using the overlap check as a key subroutine.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Sort intervals by start: [[1,2],[1,3],[2,3],[3,4]]
Step 2: Initialize dp = [1,1,1,1]
Step 3: For i=1 (interval [1,3]): Check j=0 ([1,2]): Overlap? [1,3] starts at 1, [1,2] ends at 2 → 1 < 2 → overlap → skip. dp[1] remains 1.
Step 4: For i=2 ([2,3]): Check j=0 ([1,2]): [2,3] starts at 2, [1,2] ends at 2 → 2 >= 2 → no overlap → dp[2] = max(1, dp[0]+1=2) = 2. Check j=1 ([1,3]): [2,3] starts at 2, [1,3] ends at 3 → 2 < 3 → overlap → skip. dp[2] = 2.
Step 5: For i=3 ([3,4]): Check j=0 ([1,2]): [3,4] starts at 3, [1,2] ends at 2 → 3 >= 2 → no overlap → dp[3] = max(1, dp[0]+1=2) = 2. Check j=1 ([1,3]): [3,4] starts at 3, [1,3] ends at 3 → 3 >= 3 → no overlap → dp[3] = max(2, dp[1]+1=2) = 2. Check j=2 ([2,3]): [3,4] starts at 3, [2,3] ends at 3 → 3 >= 3 → no overlap → dp[3] = max(2, dp[2]+1=3) = 3.
Step 6: Max in dp = 3. Total intervals = 4. Minimum removals = 4 - 3 = 1.

Step 1: Sort intervals by start: [[1,2],[1,2],[1,2]]
Step 2: Initialize dp = [1,1,1]
Step 3: For i=1: Check j=0: Overlap? [1,2] starts at 1, [1,2] ends at 2 → 1 < 2 → overlap → skip. dp[1]=1.
Step 4: For i=2: Check j=0: Overlap → skip. Check j=1: Overlap → skip. dp[2]=1.
Step 5: Max in dp = 1. Total intervals = 3. Minimum removals = 3 - 1 = 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Interval Overlap After Sorting
ladders.desc: Determine if two intervals overlap given they're sorted by start time
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine checks whether two intervals overlap, assuming they are sorted by start time. The intuition is that after sorting by start, for two intervals i and j where j comes before i (j < i), we only need to check if the start of i is less than the end of j to detect overlap. If intervals[i].start < intervals[j].end, they overlap; otherwise, they don't. This works because sorting ensures intervals[j].start ≤ intervals[i].start, so if intervals[i].start is before intervals[j].end, interval i starts while j is still ongoing, causing overlap. The algorithm is simple: given sorted intervals array and indices i and j, return True if intervals[i][0] < intervals[j][1], else False. Time complexity is O(1), space is O(1). This connects to L0 as it's used in the DP loop to determine if we can extend a sequence. Common mistakes include using wrong comparison (e.g., checking end times instead of start vs end) or not considering equal endpoints as non-overlap (the problem typically considers [1,2] and [2,3] as non-overlapping).

examples.trace:
Step 1: intervals[1] = [2,3], intervals[0] = [1,2]
Step 2: Check intervals[1][0] < intervals[0][1] → 2 < 2 → False
Step 3: No overlap.

Step 1: intervals[1] = [2,4], intervals[0] = [1,3]
Step 2: Check intervals[1][0] < intervals[0][1] → 2 < 3 → True
Step 3: Overlap.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP for Maximum Non-Overlapping Intervals
ladders.desc: Apply LIS-style DP to find maximum non-overlapping intervals after sorting
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves using dynamic programming to find the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals after sorting by start time. The intuition is similar to the Longest Increasing Subsequence problem, but instead of increasing values, we check for non-overlap. We define dp[i] as the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals ending at interval i. Initially, dp[i] = 1 for all i (each interval by itself). Then, for each interval i, we look at all previous intervals j (j < i). If interval i does not overlap with interval j (checked using the L1 subroutine), we can extend the sequence ending at j to include i, so dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). After processing all i, the maximum value in dp gives the maximum non-overlapping intervals we can keep. The algorithm: 1. Sort intervals by start. 2. Initialize dp array with 1s. 3. For i from 1 to n-1: For j from 0 to i-1: If no overlap between i and j: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j]+1). 4. Return max(dp). Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops, space is O(n). This connects to L0 as it computes the value needed for minimum removals. Common mistakes include not initializing dp properly, incorrect overlap check, or missing the sort step.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Sort: [[1,3],[2,4],[3,5]]
Step 2: dp = [1,1,1]
Step 3: i=1 ([2,4]): j=0 ([1,3]): Overlap? 2 < 3 → True → skip. dp[1]=1
Step 4: i=2 ([3,5]): j=0: No overlap? 3 >= 3 → True → dp[2] = max(1, 1+1=2) = 2. j=1: Overlap? 3 < 4 → True → skip. dp[2]=2
Step 5: Max dp = 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Sort Intervals by Start Time
ladders.desc: Sort a list of intervals based on their start times
ladders.explanation: This building block involves sorting an array of intervals in ascending order based on their start times. The intuition is that sorting by start time organizes intervals chronologically, which simplifies subsequent operations like overlap checks and DP. It ensures that for any two intervals i and j with i > j, intervals[i].start ≥ intervals[j].start. This property is crucial for the overlap check in L1 and the DP in L2. The algorithm is straightforward: use a sorting function (e.g., in Python, intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])) to sort the list in-place. Time complexity is O(n log n) for comparison-based sort, space is O(1) if sorted in-place. This connects to L2 as it's a prerequisite for the DP logic. Common mistakes include sorting by end time instead of start, or not sorting at all, which breaks the overlap check assumption.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Input: [[3,4],[1,2],[2,3]]
Step 2: Extract start times: [3,1,2]
Step 3: Sort by start: order indices based on start values.
Step 4: After sorting: [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4]]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Interval Start and End
ladders.desc: Retrieve the start and end values from an interval representation
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing the start and end times from an interval, typically represented as a list or tuple of two integers [start, end]. The intuition is that intervals are defined by their start and end points, and we need to extract these values for comparisons, sorting, and overlap checks. In most programming languages, if an interval is stored as an array `interval`, then `interval[0]` gives the start time and `interval[1]` gives the end time. The algorithm is simple: given an interval, return its first element as start and second as end. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used in sorting by start time. Common mistakes include using wrong indices (e.g., accessing index 1 for start) or assuming intervals have more than two elements.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Interval = [1,5]
Step 2: Access index 0: value = 1 → start
Step 3: Access index 1: value = 5 → end

Step 1: Interval = [10,20]
Step 2: Index 0 = 10 → start
Step 3: Index 1 = 20 → end

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is an Interval?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic definition and representation of an interval
ladders.explanation: An interval is a continuous range between two points, typically represented as [start, end] where start and end are numbers, and start ≤ end. In this problem, intervals represent time periods or segments. The intuition is that intervals model periods where something occurs, and overlap happens when two intervals share some common time. For example, [1,3] and [2,4] overlap because time 2 is in both. Intervals are usually given as a list of pairs. The key properties are: start and end are inclusive or exclusive based on problem statement (here, usually inclusive start, exclusive end for overlap checks like [1,2] and [2,3] not overlapping). The basic operation is to understand how to interpret [start, end]. This connects to L4 as it's the foundation for accessing start and end values. Common mistakes include misunderstanding inclusive/exclusive bounds or assuming intervals can have start > end.

examples.output: Starts at 1, ends at 5, includes times 1,2,3,4
examples.trace:
Step 1: Given [1,5]
Step 2: Start = 1, End = 5
Step 3: Includes all integers from 1 up to but not including 5 (if exclusive end): 1,2,3,4.

examples.output: Starts at 2, ends at 2, includes no time (zero-length)
examples.trace:
Step 1: [2,2]
Step 2: Start = 2, End = 2
Step 3: Since start = end, it represents a point or zero-length interval.
